candidates.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants
tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
ToolSandbox: A Stateful, Conversational, Interactive Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Tool Use Capabilities

arguments.query_text: benchmark construction human annotators GPT-4 plugins results

locations.preview: GAIA questions are conceptually simple for humans yet challenging for most advanced AIs: we show that human respondents obtain 92% vs. 15% for GPT-4 equipped with plugins.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: GPT-4 Plugins (set by humans) Human
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Table 4 Score and average time to answer for various baselines on GAIA in %. *: GPT4 + plugins scores were obtained by manually selecting plugins, and cannot be reproduced exactly for the reasons described in the main text. **: Human score corresponds to the portion of correct answers by validation annotators for valid questions.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Human non-specialist accuracy on the benchmark is only 34.5% Expert-level human performance is estimated at 89.8%.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: AgentBench 3 https://huggingface.co/blog/evaluating-mmlu-leaderboard 4 As defined in https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/BoA3agdkAzL6HQtQP/clarifying-and-predicting-agi, a t-AGI beats, on most tasks, most human experts who are given time t to perform the task
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Both figures are based on information reported by human annotators when answering the questions, and AI systems might proceed differently.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: While the questions are conceptually simple, annotators might do inadvertent mistakes: we estimate the annotator’s success rate to be 92% when aggregated on all levels of difficulty, and report this as the human score for GAIA.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Our non-LLM baselines are human annotators, and web search.

arguments.query_text: design principles data collection annotation pipeline validation steps

locations.preview: To complete a task, a system has to plan and successfully complete some number of steps since the resulting answer is absent by design in plain text from current pre-training data.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Answers are unambiguous and by design unlikely to be found in plain text in training data.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: This subsection delves into our question design and annotation process.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Once a question was created, it was also annotated, i.e. the question creator provided an answer as well as meta-data: which tools were needed, which steps were taken, or how many time was required to answer.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: But, tasks that are difficult for humans are not necessarily difficult for recent systems: the challenging MMLU or GSM8k benchmarks for example (Hendrycks et al., 2021; Cobbe et al., 2021) are already close to be solved,1 due to rapid LLM improvement possibly combined with data contamination.2 Furthermore, open-ended generation generally requires human or model-based evaluation (Zheng et al., 2023).
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: We move in that direction by proposing GAIA, a benchmark for General AI Assistants featuring 466 carefully crafted questions and their answer, along with the associated design methodology.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: For example, a LLM will typically need to browse the open and changing web, handle multi-modality, or reason over multiple steps to answer our questions.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Answering the questions requires successful completion of some number of steps, which cannot easily be brute forced due to their diversity.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: The possibility to check the reasoning trace, the accuracy required in the answers, their absence in plain text from the internet prevent a possible data contamination.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: This paper covers the composition of GAIA, its design choices, and explain how to craft questions and the associated challenges so that the community can further extend the benchmark to target emerging questions such as safety associated to tool use, or multi-modality.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

arguments.query_text: annotators recruited guidelines validation three levels difficulty

locations.preview: The questions can be sorted into three levels of increasing difficulty depending on the number of steps required to solve the questions, and the number of different tools needed to answer the question.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: While the questions are conceptually simple, annotators might do inadvertent mistakes: we estimate the annotator’s success rate to be 92% when aggregated on all levels of difficulty, and report this as the human score for GAIA.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: We estimate the creation of a question, including its validation by two supplementary annotators and potential repairs, to require two hours of annotator time.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Our proposed levels of difficulty, loosely defined in terms of number of steps and number of different capabilities used, are correlated with the performance of current models, strengthening their validity.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: We estimate the creation of a question, including its validation by two supplementary annotators and potential repairs, requires two hours of annotator time.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Conversely, many LLM benchmarks are quite specific and/or restricted to closed and synthetic environments. - Easy interpretability through conceptually simple tasks—non experts annotators exhibit a near perfect score—, associated reasoning trace, and few but highly curated questions.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: This section covers the design and content of GAIA, as well as guidelines for creating questions and associated challenges.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: If catastrophic memorization happens in spite of these mitigations, it is easy to craft new questions using the guidelines we provide in Section 3.4.

arguments.query_text: recruitment Hugging Face platform annotators guidelines tools modalities

locations.preview: OpenAGI (Ge et al., 2023) introduces both a platform and a benchmark, made of a number of multi-steps tasks across modalities and capabilities, and is closer to our work.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Therefore, we rely as a proxy on the number of steps and tools used by our annotators when crafting the questions.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face, May 2023.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: When answering the questions, annotators specified the steps that were followed and listed the tools they use.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Based on the set of tools that were mentionned by the annotators, we defined capabilities required by GAIA.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: For each capability, we report examples of corresponding tool as reported by annotators. • Web browsing: tools related to search the web and browse websites.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Indeed, the latest among them (OpenAI, 2023; Anthropic, 2023; Anil et al., 2023; Touvron et al., 2023) are fluent, knowledgeable, aligned to some extent with human preferences (Ouyang et al., 2022), and can be augmented (Mialon et al., 2023) with tools such as web browsers or code interpreters in a zero or few-shot setting (Brown et al., 2020).
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Even equipped with tools, GPT4 does not exceed a 30% success rate for the easiest of our tasks, and 0% for the hardest.

locations.preview: Therefore, we rely as a proxy on the number of steps and tools used by our annotators when crafting the questions.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Answering the questions requires successful completion of some number of steps, which cannot easily be brute forced due to their diversity.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: The restricted number of highly curated questions makes the benchmark easier to use compared to aggregated ones (Perlitz et al., 2023).
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Figure 3 Left: number of questions per capability requiring at least this capability to be solved.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: At a given location, the size of the dots are proportional to the number of questions, and only the level with the highest number of questions is displayed for readability.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: The questions can be sorted into three levels of increasing difficulty depending on the number of steps required to solve the questions, and the number of different tools needed to answer the question.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: We loosely use the following definitions to attribute a level to a question: - Level 1 questions generally require no tools, or at most one tool but no more than 5 steps. - Level 2 question generally involve more steps, roughly between 5 and 10 and combining different tools is needed. - Level 3 are questions for a near perfect general assistant, requiring to take arbitrarily long sequences of actions, use any number of tools, and access to the world in general.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Our questions are created by humans5 and aim to reflect realistic use cases of AI assistants.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Apart from puzzles, most questions were created by finding and potentially combining information from different sources of truth to produce a specific answer.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: While the questions are conceptually simple, annotators might do inadvertent mistakes: we estimate the annotator’s success rate to be 92% when aggregated on all levels of difficulty, and report this as the human score for GAIA.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

arguments.query_text: validation phase two supplementary annotators answer matches exact

locations.preview: We estimate the creation of a question, including its validation by two supplementary annotators and potential repairs, to require two hours of annotator time.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: We estimate the creation of a question, including its validation by two supplementary annotators and potential repairs, requires two hours of annotator time.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: We therefore asked two new annotators to independently answer each question.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: If the original annotator and the two new annotators arrived at the same answer, the question was validated.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: We find that two rounds of annotations are required, a first annotator making their best effort to design an unambiguous question—wich takes more time than e.g. ranking two different generations for RLHF—, and two supplementary annotators independently answering the question and disambiguating it if necessary.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: After question creation, we ask two new independent annotators to answer the questions to check it is not ambiguous.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: After two new, independent annotators answer for all crafted questions: Two new annotators agree with original answer One new annotator agree with original answer, other disagree Two new annotators disagree with original answer
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: The statistics were computed on their 1246 annotations. *: a valid question is a question for which two annotators give the same answer as the question designer, or only one annotator gives the same answer as the question designer and the other made a mistake. **: the human baseline is computed as the fraction of correct answers for all tentative on valid questions by the new annotators.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Table 4 Score and average time to answer for various baselines on GAIA in %. *: GPT4 + plugins scores were obtained by manually selecting plugins, and cannot be reproduced exactly for the reasons described in the main text. **: Human score corresponds to the portion of correct answers by validation annotators for valid questions.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Express your answer in USD with two decimal places.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: The statistics were computed on their 1246 annotations. *: a valid question is a question for which two annotators give the same answer as the question designer, or only one annotator gives the same answer as the question designer and the other made a mistake. **: the human baseline is computed as the fraction of correct answers for all tentative on valid questions by the new annotators.

locations.preview: GAIA proposes real-world questions that require a set of fundamental abilities such as reasoning, multi-modality handling, web browsing, and generally tool-use proficiency.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Conversely, many LLM benchmarks are quite specific and/or restricted to closed and synthetic environments. - Easy interpretability through conceptually simple tasks—non experts annotators exhibit a near perfect score—, associated reasoning trace, and few but highly curated questions.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Using GAIA’s methodology, we devise 466 questions and their answer.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: We move in that direction by proposing GAIA, a benchmark for General AI Assistants featuring 466 carefully crafted questions and their answer, along with the associated design methodology.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: GAIA attempts to avoid current pitfalls of LLMs evaluation by targeting: - Real-world and challenging questions.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: The possibility to check the reasoning trace, the accuracy required in the answers, their absence in plain text from the internet prevent a possible data contamination.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: In contrast, multiple choice answers (e.g., MMLU) make contamination assessment more difficult since a wrong reasoning trace can more easily get to the correct choice. - Simplicity of use.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: We release a developer set of 166 annotated questions and release the remaining 300 questions without annotations: the benchmark will be notably hosted as a leaderboard.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: This section covers the design and content of GAIA, as well as guidelines for creating questions and associated challenges.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: It is composed of 466 questions designed and annotated by humans.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants
